author: @ogi22
text:
Lovely project as always!
Just two comments, and I'm not the only one who thought about it (I have seen several similar comments so i thought of grouping them together).
- Bigger space or cavity to evacuate water, to prevent that rotor stalling. Since this is a pressure operated system, inlet could be just near the outlet, so only a minimum amount of water is dragged around the wheel. And much bigger outlet hole. This is a very turbulent flow, so the bigger out hole the better. I would even make that thing in a big "U" shape, with empty space at the bottom.
- Nozzles. Tips which can accept different size nozzles, or even better - a slot for a nozzle plate. This way you can change only a small plate with nozzles or adapt any other nozzles you can find.

Happy tinkering!

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
Technically, you can add multiple jets, assuming you have enough water flow, in pressure, and that when you consider that you need to ensure a free relief for each aperture, but that is not that terrible, should you simply allow enough space on the back side where that consumed water can find a way to escape, without needing to re-impinge upon the wheel [where it would cause viscous and kinetic drag].

In other words, a healthy bit of open space needs to be employed in the housing mechanism on this type of open turbine system, so that waste* water *[aka the water sprayed and not recoverable as more power, something that happens with all passive type reaction water wheels] has enough open space to escape, without coming back and acting against the work it just did.

One way on this is to have some baffle plates along behind the reaction points, so that the spent water is immediately diverted away from the main reaction wheel, and drained so that it can not come back and return to the wheel.

After all...

Water makes as good of a BRAKE as it does a driver, and in many cases, moreso.

While this is not a problem in positive pressure displacement motors [such as 'true' hydrostatic type vane and gear types], it is certainly an issue with free space, fall-away, passive reaction turbines.

This is one reason many such turbines are laid on their sides.

Such reaction wheels have come quite popular of recent years, and they lay on their sides, because the jets crossing the reaction wheels from the top, can easily just drain out the bottom.

They tend to be made where there is no opposing side, thus, nothing lay below them but open air, and the water can not come back up and cause drag issues.

This is a 'vertical axis' type layout, for those who may not have caught on to that point.

There are some videos of people using natural streams for this purpose on the internet, easily findable.

replies.author: @chrisyarnold6205
replies.text:
I have seen these set up with a vertical axis wheel, with 4 jets placed equidistant around the wheel, and yes, space below the wheel where gravity takes water quickly away. The generator then sits on top of the wheel, and it was very efficient.
All water supplied from a fast stream, piped down a hillside to give pressure for the injectors and returned  downstream.

replies.author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
replies.text:
@chrisyarnold6205I would build one here in the creek that adjoins my property, if I had enough land to make it actually work.

I am, instead, looking to take a very radical hydrostatic water table flow route to harvest power.

It will take a long time to make it work, but once it works, it SHOULD give me about 15 Kw  [about 20.134228188 Hp] based on the hydrological values of this location where I live, on the side of the largest peak in the region of 150 miles, based purely on volume of water, and columnar pressure.

It will take a long time to develop it, however, because it will require many, very long holes drilled into the mountain [ "long" as in over 1 kilometer each ], all tapped into a single collection port, capable of dealing with over 300 PSIG [ 2068.427184 KPA ] so that it does not burst and leak on me.

I am not just doing this for a source of steady, free, uninterruptible power, but as a way to operate my future mine, which will use almost all of that power.

author: @mooneym.3642
text: IIRC the "double cup" pelton wheel was the most efficient design. From Google: "Typically two buckets are mounted side-by-side on the wheel, with the water jet split into two equal streams; this balances the side-load forces on the wheel and helps to ensure smooth, efficient transfer of momentum from the water jet to the turbine wheel."

author: @pauldent3059
text: You would have thought the water company could have been used to provide electricity through this type of system rather than installing wires and infrastructure in the early days by having a "live" positive pressure pipe system, just imagine what could have been developed if they'd gone that route 150 years ago

replies.author: @driverjamescopeland
replies.text: Not really. The whole point was to maintain flow/energy. Any time you use said flow to power anything, you add restriction... and adding restriction, adds heat. Even on live source, gravity-fed systems... you never want to introduce unnecessary restriction/heat into the system.

replies.author: @stephenroot1012
replies.text: Unless there was a way to recover the water used for power our waste water would be three to five times as much per household by my guess. On the plus side using on site generation of electricity would have meant that we could have gone with low voltage DC ( 12 volts or so) for lights and most usage and on site higher voltage generation for things like AC, electric stoves and power tools.

replies.author: @pauldent3059
replies.text: True but then you could use the heat to heat the home

author: @grahamwalker5353
text: Been thinking about this for some time. I'm in the UK and we have gas central heating. So we have a pump, pumping water around a circuit for maybe 4 to 5 hours a day in winter time. Been trying to decide if its possible to use a water motor somewhere in the circuit to generate electricity/heat to be stored in a sand battery, with a heat exchanger to put heat back into the central heating system and hence, reduce thhe time that the gas boiler is needed. Trying to decide if its a goer or not. Anyone care to point out the obvious flaw in the theory that i can't see 😂

replies.author: @zapaholic
replies.text: Adding a water motor to your hot water circuit will require the pump's motor to use more energy. You're taking energy out of the flow of hot water by adding resistance to that flow.. At least that's how it looks to me. 🤔

replies.author: @grahamwalker5353
replies.text: ​@zapaholicYes, I see what you mean, but would it be significantly more than adding a few extra bends into the system? And would the reduction in gas usage outweigh the additional cost of electricity . These are the things I am trying to get my head around. Afraid my lack of knowledge is a problem.
